title: Everyone Gets Atrophy

jokes.text: Now that the original Michael's back, I suppose we don't need two hands-on-our-hips, pipe-up-our-rectum scolds. So you can go.
jokes.setup: Tobias has been impersonating Michael Bluth in Michael's absence
jokes.punchline: Lucille dismisses Tobias with a hilariously specific anatomical insult that doubles as a character description of both Michael and Tobias
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'pipe-up-our-rectum scolds' is absurdly specific, perfectly Lucille's withering cruelty, and does double duty: it describes Michael's uptightness while also landing as a Tobias gay-subtext joke. High character integration because only Lucille could dismiss someone this elegantly and viciously. Quotable and memorable.

jokes.text: Back to playing Tobias. Yes. Exactly, because I am still a valued family member.
jokes.setup: Lucille has just told Tobias to go back to playing Tobias, dismissing him
jokes.punchline: Tobias interprets 'playing Tobias' as validation that he's a 'valued family member,' completely missing the insult
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias obliviousness. He has been told to go away and somehow takes it as affirmation. The comedy is entirely character-driven — no one but Tobias could spin a dismissal into self-congratulation this completely. High character integration score.

jokes.text: so cruelly served to me from the back seat of my car by someone who thought I was a Lyft driver. Could have been the pink mustache.
jokes.setup: Tobias complains about being served divorce papers while being mistaken for a Lyft driver
jokes.punchline: Michael notes the pink mustache — Tobias was driving with a visible Lyft prop, making his confusion entirely self-inflicted
jokes.explanation: Sharp economy — Michael's one-line explanation ('could have been the pink mustache') perfectly deflates Tobias's grievance. The contemporary Lyft reference grounds absurdity in reality. The joke earns its laugh by having Tobias be the author of his own humiliation.

jokes.text: That's because everybody's thinking about Tony Wonder's beard. It was pink, right? I dyed it what I assumed was the color of Michael's skin.
jokes.setup: The group discusses whether the Lyft mustache was pink
jokes.punchline: Tobias reveals he dyed the fake mustache to match Michael's skin tone — an absurd artistic choice that implies he was genuinely trying to 'become' Michael down to flesh-colored facial hair
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Tobias made a 'skin-tone' mustache to play Michael is layered — it's absurd method-actor logic, it ties into the Tony Wonder plot, and it retroactively makes the Lyft confusion funnier. High character integration because Tobias's deranged commitment to performance art is entirely his own.

jokes.text: I only remember the thing about Tony because of the 'W,' and his beard was a 'W,' which I thought, you know, for Wonder, was funny. You know, it tickled me. His beard tickled me.
jokes.setup: The group discusses Tony Wonder's beard
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. insists he only remembers because of the letter 'W' branding, but then accidentally says 'his beard tickled me' — implying physical intimacy with Tony Wonder that G.O.B. is desperately rationalizing away
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes. G.O.B.'s tortured attempt to provide a heterosexual alibi for remembering Tony Wonder ('it was the W') collapses spectacularly when he admits 'his beard tickled me.' The joke works on multiple levels: G.O.B.'s denial, the physical intimacy implied, and the callback to their ongoing homoerotic entanglement. Earns its 8+ because only G.O.B. could deliver this specific brand of overexplained self-incrimination.

jokes.text: And is that part of what you look so confused about? It is on the list.
jokes.setup: The family has been discussing a cascade of absurd information — Tobias impersonating Michael, pink mustaches, Tony Wonder's beard tickling G.O.B.
jokes.punchline: Michael asks if all this is part of what someone looks confused about; the response 'It is on the list' implies there is a running tally of confusing things, suggesting the absurdity is quantified and ongoing
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — 'It is on the list' does enormous work in four words, implying a whole catalog of Bluth insanity that predates this moment. The understatement is sharp and the structure is tight.

jokes.text: As a matter of fact, I am. I want to be part of the problem.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Lindsay is running for office
jokes.punchline: Instead of the expected 'part of the solution,' Lindsay announces she wants to be 'part of the problem' — either through startling self-awareness or complete political naivety
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and structure. 'I want to be part of the problem' inverts the political cliché so cleanly and characterizes Lindsay perfectly — whether read as self-aware cynicism or oblivious sincerity, it works. Among the episode's most quotable lines. Justifies 8+ because it's a genuinely well-crafted subversion with strong character specificity.

jokes.text: You're intelligent and you're wonderful and ethical and you're great. I mean, don't worry. She's jealous.
jokes.setup: Lindsay worries Lucille saw compromising photos of her with a politician
jokes.punchline: George Sr. responds with flattery so transparently false (Lindsay has never been described this way by anyone) that 'she's jealous' lands as the punchline — a ridiculous non-sequitur that reveals George Sr. just saying whatever will keep Lindsay useful
jokes.explanation: The comedy comes from knowing George Sr.'s manipulation and Lindsay's neediness. The praise is so uncharacteristically effusive that it telegraphs manipulation, which is itself the joke. Competent character comedy but not outstanding.

jokes.text: It's probably just because I'm younger. A puta.
jokes.setup: Lindsay theorizes Lucille is jealous of her because she's younger
jokes.punchline: Someone off-screen (likely a Spanish speaker) says 'a puta' (a whore) — either agreeing with Lindsay's theory sarcastically or mishearing something. The joke is the juxtaposition of Lindsay's vanity with the blunt Spanish insult.
jokes.explanation: Quick, economical joke that lands on the unexpected Spanish vulgarity. The show's willingness to drop 'puta' casually as a punchline is characteristically Arrested Development — unexpected and slightly dark. Loses points for structure since context is somewhat unclear without visuals.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And perhaps Lindsay would have gotten a little healthier... had the cell reception been a little worse.' — Lindsay immediately gets a call from Lucille.
jokes.setup: The narrator muses that Lindsay might have changed had her circumstances been slightly different
jokes.punchline: Lucille calls at that exact moment, derailing any chance at growth — the cell reception is the only thing between Lindsay and self-improvement
jokes.explanation: The narrator's ironic framing ('a little healthier... had the cell reception been a little worse') is clever AD-style editorializing. The timing — Lucille calling immediately — is good structural comedy. The joke is that the universe conspires to prevent any Bluth from growing.

jokes.text: Lucille 2 is not going to be a problem. And Sally hasn't been seen since Cinco.
jokes.setup: Lucille is encouraging Lindsay to run against Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: Lucille casually notes that Lucille 2 won't be a problem and that Sally Sitwell hasn't been seen since Cinco de Cuatro — dropping two missing-person hints with chilling nonchalance
jokes.explanation: Lucille's breezy delivery of what is essentially a confession or cover-up is classic dark comedy. The joke rewards rewatching because the implication darkens considerably as the season progresses. Character integration is high — only Lucille would discuss potential foul play as a scheduling convenience.

jokes.text: Somebody say 'Wonder'? Not wonder. No one's wondering! Just Tony.
jokes.setup: Sally is trying to quietly get help while fleeing
jokes.punchline: Tony Wonder appears because he heard the word 'wonder' — his name has become a comedic trigger that summons him inappropriately, and the rapid clarifications ('not wonder / no one's wondering') escalate the confusion
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay-based running gag — Tony Wonder's name contains a common word, making him perpetually summoned by accident. The rapid back-and-forth ('not wonder / no one's wondering') is well-structured escalation. Characteristically Arrested Development in its willingness to have a joke hinge on a character's surname.

jokes.text: I hate everything about my life, including you.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and G.O.B. are in Mexico, failing to attract women
jokes.punchline: 'Including you' extends what could be general existential despair into a specific, targeted insult at G.O.B. — the casual precision of 'including you' is the comedic beat
jokes.explanation: Economical and character-true. George Sr.'s particular brand of existential misery that always finds a specific target is well-captured in this line. 'Including you' does the work — without it, it's a generic complaint; with it, it's a perfectly aimed missile.

jokes.text: I feel like one of these bulls on my breasts.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is wearing a bull-decorated shirt, lamenting his life in Mexico
jokes.punchline: He identifies with a bull printed on his shirt, leading into an extended metaphor — the visual absurdity of a man having a philosophical crisis about embroidered livestock on his chest
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'the bulls on my breasts' is inherently comic — 'breasts' used for a man's chest while describing shirt decorations creates a mild absurdity. Functions as setup for the bull monologue that follows. Works but not a standout moment.

jokes.text: His whole life, he's bred to be the strongest and the toughest and he's got to have the biggest balls... So when you're in your prime, they can drug you and throw you out in front of a crowd of people who just want to see you die so some guy in crushed velvet pants can come out and just stick a sword in you.
jokes.setup: George Sr. describes the life of a bull as a metaphor for his own experience
jokes.punchline: The monologue escalates from virility to drugging, public spectacle, and death — a shockingly bleak self-portrait delivered as if about livestock. 'Crushed velvet pants' is a perfect absurdist specific detail.
jokes.explanation: The bull monologue builds well — it starts with virility anxiety and ends in a surprisingly dark place. 'Crushed velvet pants' is the kind of specific comic detail that elevates the whole speech. George Sr.'s self-pity given metaphorical voice through bullfighting is strong character comedy. The escalation from 'biggest balls' to 'stick a sword in you' earns its darkness.

jokes.text: You're a bull who wants to stick it in everybody, and everybody ends up sticking it in you.
jokes.setup: The bull metaphor monologue has been building
jokes.punchline: The line completes the metaphor with a double entendre — 'stick it in' works both as bullfighting violence and sexual innuendo, providing a capping joke to the speech
jokes.explanation: The capping line of the bull monologue delivers the double meaning efficiently and memorably. The parallel structure ('wants to stick it in everybody / everybody ends up sticking it in you') is well-constructed and gives the long setup a satisfying payoff. Quotable and structurally sound.

jokes.text: I tried to buy those velvet pants. But I chickened out and got these shirts instead.
jokes.setup: George Sr. just mentioned 'crushed velvet pants' as the matador's costume
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. reveals he almost bought the exact pants from George Sr.'s bleak metaphor, humanizing the abstract monologue by confirming G.O.B. had a genuine velvet pant shopping moment
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. G.O.B. taking George Sr.'s dark metaphorical speech and responding with a literal shopping confession is perfect character comedy. It deflates the pathos immediately, reveals G.O.B.'s priorities (he wanted the velvet pants), and the pivot to 'I chickened out and got these shirts instead' implies his whole tacky Mexico wardrobe is a substitute for his true desire. Earns 8+ because the character integration is near-perfect and the economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: I have a friend, this guy Tony... not a friend. He's nice. He had a face like mine... but same.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. begins to open up about Tony Wonder
jokes.punchline: The self-corrections ('not a friend. He's nice.') and the baffling syntax ('a face like mine... but same') perfectly capture G.O.B.'s inability to process his feelings — 'a face like mine but same' is almost poetic in its confused tautology
jokes.explanation: Among the best jokes in the episode. The rapid self-correction ('not a friend. He's nice.') reveals G.O.B.'s emotional confusion about Tony Wonder with devastating efficiency. 'He had a face like mine... but same' is a brilliantly absurd tautology — he started to say Tony looked like him, then corrected it to say they look the same, which means the same thing. The syntax is broken in a way that perfectly mirrors G.O.B.'s broken emotional processing. High character integration because this precise flavor of confused denial is uniquely G.O.B.

jokes.text: ♪ I started a joke ♪ Hello, darkness. ♪ Which started the whole world crying ♪ Sorry, I'm taken.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. is emotionally spiraling while music plays
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. addresses the song 'I Started a Joke' as if it were a person hitting on him, saying 'Hello, darkness' and 'Sorry, I'm taken' — talking back to music as a coping mechanism while also inadvertently referencing Simon & Garfunkel's 'The Sound of Silence'
jokes.explanation: G.O.B. talking back to a Bee Gees song as if it's a suitor ('Sorry, I'm taken') while also accidentally greeting darkness Simon-and-Garfunkel-style is wonderfully layered. It works as pure character comedy (G.O.B.'s narcissism extends to being flattered by songs) and as absurdist non sequitur. High character integration.

jokes.text: Oh, cry me a blizzard, snowflake. I gotta run.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. expresses self-pity about not being wanted
jokes.punchline: 'Cry me a blizzard, snowflake' is a layered insult — it extends 'cry me a river' (blizzard > river of tears) while also using 'snowflake' as a political insult for the oversensitive, aimed by George Sr. at his son
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best crafted one-liners. 'Cry me a blizzard, snowflake' achieves multiple things simultaneously: it escalates the familiar idiom ('river' to 'blizzard'), deploys 'snowflake' as a contemporary political insult, and the two meteorological words create an internal coherence. Maximum funny per word. Justifies 8+ easily — this is the kind of line that gets quoted.

jokes.text: George Sr. tells G.O.B. his mother called him back: 'My God. Must be nice to be wanted, even if it's just your mom.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. announces he's been called back — his mother wants him. G.O.B. reacts
jokes.punchline: G.O.B.'s line reveals his total loneliness and mommy-neediness while he pretends to be above it ('even if it's just your mom')
jokes.explanation: Good G.O.B. character beat — his desperate need to be wanted is expressed through fake condescension. Functional but not particularly innovative.

jokes.text: Look who's horny now.
jokes.setup: George Sr. has just been energized by a phone call from Lucille telling him he's needed
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. observes George Sr.'s sudden energy by calling it horniness — reversing their earlier conversation where George Sr. complained 'Daddy not horny,' using 'horny' both as vitality and libido
jokes.explanation: Tight callback to the earlier 'Daddy not horny' exchange. Extremely economical — three words close the loop on an earlier joke. The ambiguity of 'horny' (energized vs. sexually aroused) does comic work. Solid construction.

jokes.text: I'm running for Congress, and I don't have a campaign manager and I need you. / Maeby had never heard her mother this vulnerable. Yeah. Sounds great. I'm in.
jokes.setup: Lindsay calls Maeby and says she needs her — the narrator notes Maeby had 'never heard her mother this vulnerable'
jokes.punchline: Maeby agrees immediately, but we've just seen her declare that people can't resist 'taking advantage of the truly vulnerable' — so her enthusiasm is entirely predatory, not filial
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural callback — within the same scene, Maeby's agreement to help her mother is immediately recontextualized by the narrator cutting to her earlier declaration about exploiting vulnerability. The joke requires the juxtaposition to land, and the show executes it well. High character integration because Maeby as a con artist exploiting her own mother is entirely consistent with her arc.

jokes.text: Hey. It is hard to resist. Taking advantage of the vulnerable.
jokes.setup: Maeby has just agreed to be Lindsay's campaign manager; the narrator observes her motivation
jokes.punchline: The line and narration confirm Maeby's response was entirely calculated — the callback lands as the narrator essentially calls out Maeby's con
jokes.explanation: The narrator making the subtext explicit while Maeby herself confirms it is a solid structural move. Works as character comedy and irony. Part of the same joke as #24 but distinct enough to stand alone as the landing beat.

jokes.text: Oh, my gosh. I didn't even recognize you. It's a teenager thing. I could never pull that off. They want to look old, we want to look... You don't look very young.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is about to make a generic comment about teenagers wanting to look old while adults want to look young
jokes.punchline: She gets cut off with 'You don't look very young' — a blunt, rude interjection that deflates her before she can deliver her own vanity speech
jokes.explanation: Sharp comedic beat — Lindsay is in the middle of setting up a self-serving comparison that flatters her, and she's cut off with a direct contradiction. The interruption structure works well and the bluntness is funny.

jokes.text: See, she used to be an Orange County weather girl, which is just tits, teeth and ass, right? Now she's the host of O.C. Can You Say, which is tits, teeth, public affairs, ass, some light interviews. I mean, it's so much more responsibility!
jokes.setup: A family member is explaining who the woman presenting their award is
jokes.punchline: He describes the weather girl role as 'tits, teeth and ass,' then expands her current role to the same formula with 'public affairs' and 'some light interviews' inserted in the middle — treating objectification as a career ladder
jokes.explanation: The structural joke of upgrading 'tits, teeth and ass' to 'tits, teeth, public affairs, ass, some light interviews' while treating this as a serious career advancement is sharp. The format creates the expectation and the 'I mean, it's so much more responsibility!' sells it as character irony — the speaker is oblivious to how inappropriate his description is.

jokes.text: She was married to that news anchor who said such horrible things about me when I was trying to save Buster with that boat. John Beard. Yeah. She's my Beard now.
jokes.setup: Lucille references the news anchor John Beard and his wife
jokes.punchline: 'She's my Beard now' — Lucille uses 'beard' as both a surname (John Beard's wife becoming 'her beard') and the slang term for a person who provides cover for a secret, applied to the TV host presenting their award
jokes.explanation: Outstanding wordplay that works on two levels: 'beard' as John Beard's surname (his ex-wife is literally A Beard) and 'beard' as slang for someone providing cover. Lucille casually sliding into this double meaning with zero acknowledgment is perfect for her character. Economy is exceptional — the punchline is three words. Earns 8+ because it requires setup that pays off brilliantly and it's the kind of layered pun the show is famous for.

jokes.text: Our Lindsay. I've never been less ashamed of her. Did I say what I think I said? Yes, you did.
jokes.setup: Lucille wants to express pride in Lindsay's political candidacy
jokes.punchline: 'I've never been less ashamed of her' — Lucille's version of a compliment is framed as shame reduction rather than actual pride. She then questions herself, and George Sr. confirms the Freudian slip was accurate.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'I've never been less ashamed of her' is a pitch-perfect Lucille Bluth compliment — entirely framed in shame and its quantification, incapable of positive framing. The structure is excellent: statement, Lucille's dawning awareness, George Sr.'s confirmation. Could ONLY come from Lucille and perfectly characterizes the Bluth parent-child dynamic. Earns 8+ comfortably.

jokes.text: She flew the coop. In the middle of running for Congress. I mean, no one remembers seeing her since Cinco de Cuatro.
jokes.setup: The family is asked about Lucille Austero's whereabouts
jokes.punchline: The casual delivery of 'she flew the coop' for a potentially murdered woman, with 'no one remembers seeing her since Cinco' — treating a likely murder as a scheduling inconvenience
jokes.explanation: The dark comedy of treating a potential murder as a minor political inconvenience is consistent with Arrested Development's tone. 'Flew the coop' as a euphemism for possible death is suitably grim. Scores decently on rewatch value because the full implication only lands once you know the season's mystery.

jokes.text: It was during the 1992 African-American riots, and they had every right. Every right. Every right. Mm.
jokes.setup: The Bluths are explaining why they don't want cameras in their house
jokes.punchline: George Sr. and Lucille defensively insist rioters 'had every right' — a politically performative statement that reads as entirely hollow coming from the Bluths, who are almost certainly not allies of any progressive cause
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the Bluths' performative wokeness — the repetition ('Every right. Every right. Mm.') suggests they're overcorrecting, protesting too much. Strong character integration because the Bluths professing progressive values is inherently ironic given their established characters.

jokes.text: Tell you what is hard beyond belief. My God, I wish you could get a feel on these two things that Joni's got going over here. They're like two geodes! You can feel them. I can't. My go-to joke is that these are the best two things John Beard's money can buy.
jokes.setup: Michael has just said something is 'hard beyond belief'; Barry takes it sexually
jokes.punchline: Barry pivots the conversation to obsessing over the TV host's implants, calling them 'geodes' and revealing his 'go-to joke' about them — characterizing Barry as someone with a rehearsed bit about his girlfriend's plastic surgery
jokes.explanation: 'Geodes' as a descriptor for breast implants is an unexpected and specific word choice that elevates an otherwise lowbrow joke. 'Go-to joke' implies Barry has thought about this enough to workshop a bit, which is characterful. The sexual pivot off 'hard beyond belief' is obvious but competently executed.

jokes.text: I'm probably grumpy because I was out late last night, raping and murdering. Not cool. I wouldn't joke about that. I rape and murder, and I joke.
jokes.setup: George Michael apologizes for being grumpy to a waiter who points out they're in Mexico
jokes.punchline: George Michael jokes about 'raping and murdering' — referencing Trump's infamous 2015 comments about Mexican immigrants. The waiter's straight-faced rebuke ('I wouldn't joke about that') flips the racial dynamic. George Michael's response ('I rape and murder, and I joke') is a non-sequitur escalation.
jokes.explanation: A pointed political joke that works on multiple levels — the Trump reference is timely (the show flags this with '2015' later), the role reversal of the Mexican waiter being offended by an American joking about it is sharp, and George Michael's deadpan 'I rape and murder, and I joke' is surreal. The show acknowledges its own edge with the narrator's later note about 2015 standards. High originality for a sitcom political gag.

jokes.text: Hello? Here to clean the windows! / Total regression!
jokes.setup: George Michael returns to his empty childhood home
jokes.punchline: 'Here to clean the windows' — George Michael gives a fake excuse to an empty house, suggesting even he knows he shouldn't be here. The narrator's 'Total regression!' confirms the joke.
jokes.explanation: The detail that George Michael announces himself to an empty house ('Here to clean the windows!') as a reflex excuse is a sharp character observation — he's pre-justifying his regression even to no one. The narrator's enthusiastic 'Total regression!' frames it perfectly.

jokes.text: God, it's so much smaller than I remember.
jokes.setup: George Michael has returned to his childhood home / bedroom
jokes.punchline: The classic 'everything looks smaller when you're grown up' observation — played straight but given resonance by George Michael's specific regression narrative
jokes.explanation: The weakest joke in the sequence — it's a completely generic observation that could come from any character in any show about returning home. Low originality because this observation has been made in dozens of TV shows and films. Functional but uninspired.

jokes.text: For scenario two, you just found out that your Darth Vader figure is dating your girlfriend, and you find out he knew she was your... Whatever. Who gives a shit?
jokes.setup: George Michael is apparently teaching a self-defense or confidence course
jokes.punchline: His scenario for students to 'apply to themselves' is transparently his own situation — Darth Vader = Michael (his father), the girlfriend = Rebel. He breaks off the scenario mid-sentence with 'Whatever. Who gives a shit?' — revealing how raw the wound still is
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy. George Michael is supposedly instructing a class but his 'scenario' is nakedly autobiographical — Darth Vader (his father figure / Michael), the girlfriend (Rebel). The breakdown into 'Whatever. Who gives a shit?' mid-instruction is perfect — it shows he can't maintain the professional distance. Paired with the narrator noting 'as usual, guys, just apply your own scenarios,' it's clear he always does this. Earns 8+ for character integration and the earned emotional underpinning.

jokes.text: He even inspired a certain move that would eventually appear two years later in a Han Solo origin picture.
jokes.setup: George Michael is teaching some kind of movement or fight technique
jokes.punchline: The narrator claims George Michael's move was copied by a Han Solo movie — a self-referential joke about Michael Cera's career and the Star Wars franchise, implying the Solo filmmakers stole from George Michael Bluth
jokes.explanation: A sharp meta-joke that works on character (George Michael's unlikely influence on pop culture) and as a real-world wink (the timing of Season 5 vs. Solo: A Star Wars Story). The show's willingness to use the narrator to make real-world pop culture jabs through the character's story is distinctly Arrested Development.

jokes.text: There. That way... She can figure it out.
jokes.setup: George Michael has spent excessive time crafting a response to Maeby's text
jokes.punchline: After all that deliberation, he sends something so cryptic that 'she can figure it out' — refusing to be emotionally clear even in a text, passing the emotional labor to Maeby
jokes.explanation: George Michael's emotional avoidance expressed through over-engineered vagueness is consistent characterization. The joke is that the deliberation produced deliberately unclear output — maximum effort for minimum communication. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Hi... don't like it. / What? My hair? No, no, I like it. I just, uh, don't like that I'm just seeing it now.
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives and sees Maeby in a grey wig
jokes.punchline: His 'Hi... don't like it' is misread as referring to her wig; he corrects that he likes the hair but doesn't like that he wasn't told about it sooner — revealing he's disappointed to have missed out on seeing her as an old woman
jokes.explanation: The rapid clarification structure works — 'I like it, I just don't like that I'm seeing it now' reframes his complaint in a way that's stranger than the original. The cringe comedy of two cousins having this hair conversation is present but understated.

jokes.text: I guess I was picturing you at 16. But I-I don't know why I... do that. But why did you do that?
jokes.setup: George Michael tries to explain why he reacted to Maeby's appearance
jokes.punchline: 'I guess I was picturing you at 16' — George Michael unconsciously admits to picturing his cousin in her teenage form, then immediately catches himself and can't explain it, while Maeby turns it back on him
jokes.explanation: Deeply uncomfortable in the best Arrested Development way — George Michael admitting he pictures his cousin as a teenager, then faltering mid-sentence, is rich character comedy loaded with subtext about their complicated relationship. 'I don't know why I do that' is a perfect self-incriminating half-correction. High character integration because this specific dynamic belongs entirely to George Michael and Maeby.

jokes.text: Well, truthfully... No, I'll just tell you the truth: it's a wig.
jokes.setup: George Michael asks why Maeby looks different; she starts to be 'truthful' then pivots
jokes.punchline: 'No, I'll just tell you the truth: it's a wig' — she interrupted herself starting to say 'truthfully' to announce she'll say the truth instead, suggesting there was a version of 'truthful' that wasn't actually the truth
jokes.explanation: Maeby's self-interruption — abandoning 'truthfully' to announce 'I'll just tell you the truth' — is a subtle joke about how truth is something she has to consciously decide to deploy. Good character economy for a con artist. The punchline (it's a wig) is functional and clears the way for the real explanation.

jokes.text: Everything I've given her to do or say has only made her go up in the polls. It's like the more offensive it is, the more people get behind her.
jokes.setup: Maeby explains her campaign strategy for Lindsay
jokes.punchline: The explicit observation that offensive behavior increases poll numbers is a pointed satire of 2015-era U.S. politics — the joke requires no subtlety because the reality was itself absurd
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire but somewhat on-the-nose — the show is making the Trump analogy explicit, which is amusing but not surprising. The joke lands because of cultural recognition rather than particularly clever construction.

jokes.text: She had her mother mock a reporter who had a speech problem... This so-called reporter from the Wee Guardian says I was right.
jokes.setup: Maeby engineered offensive political moments; the narrator describes one involving a speech-impaired reporter
jokes.punchline: Lindsay mocking a reporter's speech impediment (a direct Trump reference) is presented as a campaign tactic — the show makes the analogy explicit with Wee Britain's 'Wee Guardian' newspaper
jokes.explanation: The Wee Britain callback adds a layer for longtime fans. The political satire is pointed but relies heavily on cultural reference rather than construction. Functional satirical comedy.

jokes.text: Although, remember, this was in 2015, and many of the offensive comments almost seem quaint by today's standards.
jokes.setup: The narrator describes Lindsay's controversial campaign moments
jokes.punchline: The narrator breaks frame to note that 2015 political offensiveness is 'quaint by today's standards' — a real-world acknowledgment of how politics escalated between the events depicted and the show's airing
jokes.explanation: The show's narrator stepping outside the fiction to make a direct commentary on political reality is sharp and meta. 'Quaint by today's standards' is an economical, devastating observation. The joke works because it's both accurate and depressing. High originality for a network-adjacent comedy to be this explicit.

jokes.text: While shocking, the video seems to have only given her a big rise in the polls. It certainly had that effect on me. - Not necessary! - Don't sack me.
jokes.setup: A reporter notes that Lindsay's offensive hot mic tape increased her poll numbers
jokes.punchline: The reporter adds 'It certainly had that effect on me' (a sexual double entendre about 'big rise'), Maeby objects, and the reporter pleads not to be fired ('Don't sack me') using British slang — the reporter is apparently British and using 'sack' which itself sounds vulgar in context
jokes.explanation: 'Don't sack me' inadvertently extending the sexual innuendo is a nice bit of comic escalation. The reporter's own innuendo followed by his plea not to lose his job has a flustered quality that works. Not exceptional but competently assembled.

jokes.text: Yeah. That's why I came up with the idea for us to kiss. / So the kissing is part of a plan?
jokes.setup: Maeby explains her campaign strategy of generating attention
jokes.punchline: She reveals the kissing is a campaign tactic — George Michael's horror ('So the kissing is part of a plan?') suggests he had hoped it was genuine, which is simultaneously uncomfortable (cousins) and poignant
jokes.explanation: The cringe comedy of George Michael being disappointed that their cousin-kissing was merely strategic is rich. His question 'So the kissing is part of a plan?' reveals he wanted it to be real, which makes it both funnier and more uncomfortable. High character integration — this specific emotional trap belongs only to this relationship.

jokes.text: And then, I open my mouth, and I have these rotted out meth teeth. And I'm like, 'Then why do I gotta my cousin for some meth money, Mom?' You know, or something like that.
jokes.setup: Maeby is planning how to use Lindsay's speech against her
jokes.punchline: Maeby's sabotage plan involves fake meth teeth and demanding meth money from her mother on camera — an escalation to absurdity that's also strangely specific and reveals Maeby's relationship with transgression
jokes.explanation: 'Rotted out meth teeth' as a planned prop for sabotaging a family awards ceremony is the kind of specific, escalated absurdity that AD excels at. 'Then why do I gotta my cousin for some meth money' is a grammatically broken sentence that works because Maeby is making it up on the spot. Characterful dark comedy.

jokes.text: Well, at least I didn't punch her in the face.
jokes.setup: George Michael comments on Maeby's plan being 'unnecessarily cruel'
jokes.punchline: 'At least I didn't punch her in the face' — George Michael sets the bar for acceptable behavior very low, implying this has been discussed or he's genuinely reassuring himself he hasn't done worse
jokes.explanation: Economical callback — 'at least I didn't punch her in the face' reminds us George Michael has punched someone (his father) without making it explicit. The low bar of 'not punching someone' as a moral victory is darkly funny and perfectly delivered as a deadpan self-reassurance.

jokes.text: You know our family motto. We forget, but we never forgive. I remember the framed needlepoint.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if George Sr. and Lucille have resolved their issues
jokes.punchline: George Sr. inverts 'forgive and forget' to 'we forget, but we never forgive' — a perfect inversion that characterizes the Bluths' specific brand of dysfunction, and Michael confirming 'I remember the framed needlepoint' suggests this is literally a family motto on display
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. 'We forget, but we never forgive' is a genuinely brilliant inversion of the cliché — it captures the exact pathology of the Bluth family (they lose track of grievances but maintain pure, sourceless resentment), and Michael's straight-faced 'I remember the framed needlepoint' suggests this is literally institutionalized family doctrine. The needlepoint detail is the perfect specificity that elevates it from clever to exceptional. This is a cultural touchstone-level joke for AD fans. Structurally perfect, maximally economical, only works for THIS family.

jokes.text: Although, by not forgiving, you're throwing everything out and just keeping the hate. Well, how do you think she keeps that figure?
jokes.setup: Michael points out the flaw in 'we forget but we never forgive'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. responds that Lucille's hate-fueled physique is maintained by the hate itself — 'how do you think she keeps that figure?' treats sustained resentment as a diet plan
jokes.explanation: Excellent follow-through joke. George Sr. extending the 'keeping the hate' observation to explain Lucille's figure — 'how do you think she keeps that figure?' — is a perfect tag. It takes Michael's analysis and turns it into a specific Lucille observation. Earns 8+ because it builds on an already-strong joke and adds a new dimension rather than just coasting.

jokes.text: Oh! Everyone gets a trophy, huh, Pop? Well, maybe I... did something right, huh? But I was like, 'But you gave one to everybody else. What happened to everybody gets a trophy?' There was that then. It was getting started, but...
jokes.setup: G.O.B. is complaining about the family award, and the conversation turns to whether he deserved recognition as a child
jokes.punchline: George Sr. deflects by saying maybe he 'did something right,' but G.O.B. reveals the wound: George Sr. gave trophies to everyone else while denying G.O.B. one — the title of the episode ('Everyone Gets Atrophy') is revealed to be a play on this childhood grievance
jokes.explanation: The episode title joke reveals itself here — 'Everyone Gets Atrophy' puns on 'everyone gets a trophy.' G.O.B.'s wounded recounting of being denied trophies while others received them is classic character vulnerability dressed as complaint. The long, rambling nature loses economy points.

jokes.text: Meanwhile, the guy who could've tried harder is now giving it to the not super young, obviously, but still fairly well known local TV host whose breasts... And you're gonna be surprised to hear this coming from me... May be a little bit too hard.
jokes.setup: G.O.B. is complaining about his father's hypocrisy regarding trophies
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. pivots from family grievances to commenting on the TV host's breast implants — framed as a surprising admission ('you're gonna be surprised to hear this coming from me'), revealing G.O.B. has a threshold for breast firmness that is apparently well established
jokes.explanation: The callback to the implants subplot works, and G.O.B. presenting having breast preferences as a surprising disclosure is funny character work. 'Not super young, obviously, but still fairly well known local TV host' is a wonderfully qualified insult. Loses economy points for the lengthy qualification.

jokes.text: Fine, I'll get trophies for everyone. Everyone gets a trophy. Count me out. Fine. Everyone but G.O.B. No!
jokes.setup: The family argues about who gets a trophy at the awards ceremony
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. opts out of the trophy, Lucille agrees he won't get one, and then G.O.B. protests ('No!') — he opted out and immediately demands back in, capturing his mercurial neediness
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic structure. G.O.B. declares himself out, Lucille accepts it, and G.O.B. immediately protests — three beats, three laughs, all landing. The 'No!' at the end is the perfect button. This encapsulates G.O.B.'s entire personality: wanting exclusion to be on his terms, unable to handle being excluded by others. Very strong character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Sometimes, the truth can hurt a man more than the most brutally fashioned lie.
jokes.setup: George Michael suggests G.O.B. just tell Michael the truth about Rebel
jokes.punchline: G.O.B. delivers this quasi-wisdom as if it's sage advice, but it's actually just a tortured way of saying he doesn't want to be honest — G.O.B. fashioning his cowardice as philosophy
jokes.explanation: G.O.B.'s self-serving pseudo-wisdom is well-captured here. 'The most brutally fashioned lie' is a wonderfully overwrought phrase that reveals how G.O.B. has romanticized his own deceptions. The joke is that this is meant to sound like wisdom but is just G.O.B. trying to justify dishonesty.

jokes.text: Is anyone else noticing that the family isn't quite, uh, as we remember them? In what way? That is not Buster.
jokes.setup: Michael is observing the assembled family
jokes.punchline: Someone points out 'that is not Buster' — confirming Tobias is still playing Buster, and no one noticed until Michael's outside perspective flagged it
jokes.explanation: The reveal that the family accepted Tobias-as-Buster without question until an outsider pointed it out is a nice joke about the Bluths' inattentiveness to each other. The delivery is appropriately low-key.

jokes.text: Did the skit start? I don't have the hook thing. Hey, what's happening? Can I just do this with my hand instead, Tobias? Michael. I'm Michael.
jokes.setup: Tobias has been using a young actor to play Buster; the actor arrives and is confused
jokes.punchline: The young actor calls Tobias by his real name ('Tobias?') and Tobias insists 'I'm Michael' — still in character as Michael Bluth, creating a nested confusion of real/fictional identities
jokes.explanation: The cascading identity confusion — Tobias playing Michael, an actor playing Buster, and the actor breaking the fourth wall by addressing Tobias by his actual name — is well-constructed meta comedy. 'I'm Michael' insists Tobias, while clearly being neither Michael Bluth nor Michael Cera. Strong layering.

jokes.text: He's a young actor. I don't really want to be an actor. It's not what I want to do.
jokes.setup: Tobias defends the young actor he hired to play Buster
jokes.punchline: The young actor himself says he doesn't actually want to be an actor — deflating Tobias's validation of him while also serving as a meta-joke about actors in Hollywood (and possibly Tobias himself)
jokes.explanation: The kid hired to be an actor not wanting to be an actor while Tobias is a non-working actor desperately wanting to be one is a nice ironic reversal. Economy is good — the contradiction is established quickly.

jokes.text: I'm going in and out of Michael like a cuckoo clock bird.
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to maintain his character as Michael Bluth while responding to real-life events
jokes.punchline: 'Going in and out of Michael like a cuckoo clock bird' is an accidentally sexual metaphor — Tobias's usual oblivious double-entendre — applied to method acting
jokes.explanation: A textbook Tobias double-entendre. 'Going in and out of Michael like a cuckoo clock bird' is an innocently intended acting metaphor that sounds unmistakably sexual, and the image of a cuckoo clock bird adds the perfect absurdist specificity. This is exactly the kind of joke Tobias is the only character who can deliver — no one else has his specific combination of dramatic sincerity and sexual obliviousness. Justifies 8+ easily.

jokes.text: I'm Michael, G.O.B. is G.O.B., Michael is no one. And opening night energy, Places and re... Peas and carrots. Red leather...
jokes.setup: Tobias tries to re-clarify the roles everyone is playing
jokes.punchline: He concludes with actor warm-up exercises ('Peas and carrots. Red leather...') as if this family crisis is a theatrical production — breaking into physical warm-up is the wrong tool for the situation
jokes.explanation: Tobias snapping into theatrical mode mid-crisis is perfect character comedy. 'Peas and carrots' and 'Red leather' are genuine actor vocal exercises, making this specific and real rather than generic. Only Tobias would respond to a family emergency with diction drills.

jokes.text: Tobias shouts acting warmups: 'Peas and carrots. Red leather...' — Michael cuts him off.
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to direct the family performance, getting increasingly theatrical
jokes.punchline: The theatrical warmup phrases ('peas and carrots' is a classic background actor mumble; 'red leather' is a diction exercise) are charmingly misapplied to this family farce
jokes.explanation: A mild character beat for Tobias-as-director. The warmup phrases are recognizable to theater people and land as character comedy but don't have much punch on their own.

jokes.text: I mean, that guy obviously has a giant rubber hand, and I have a giant melted hand that smells like Bounce.
jokes.setup: Buster is at the police station looking at footage of someone they think might be him
jokes.punchline: Buster distinguishes himself from the suspect by clarifying that his hand 'smells like Bounce' (fabric softener) — a brand-specific detail that implies Buster maintains his prosthetic hand with laundry products, which is both absurd and pathetically specific
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'My hand smells like Bounce' is an absurdly specific detail that's entirely character-true for Buster — meticulous, slightly unhinged, and offering an identification detail no one asked for. The contrast between 'rubber hand' (generic) and 'melted hand that smells like Bounce' (Buster's specific reality) is perfect. Only Buster would have a Bounce-scented prosthetic and volunteer this information unprompted.

jokes.text: I'm just surprised to see it, since I'm pretty sure I erased that video.
jokes.setup: Police are showing Buster footage they believe implicates him
jokes.punchline: Buster's concern is not that he's on the video — it's that he thought he deleted it — immediately and casually confirming he did something worth erasing
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark comedy. Buster's non-sequitur self-incrimination — not defending himself but wondering why his deleted video still exists — is both perfectly in character and a brilliant comedic beat. The economy is excellent, the structure is a clean misdirection, and the dark implication (he tried to hide evidence) is handled with perfect lightness. Earns 8+ for all five craft criteria performing well simultaneously.

jokes.text: We don't need a bad cop. Go home. See your kid. Thanks, Rick. That's why you're the good cop.
jokes.setup: A police interaction where the dynamics of 'good cop / bad cop' are referenced
jokes.punchline: One officer sends the 'bad cop' home to see his kid as a nice gesture — the 'bad cop' is treated with warmth, and the good cop designates the kind action as what makes the other 'the good cop,' creating a paradox
jokes.explanation: The inversion of good cop/bad cop designations is mildly amusing but not particularly original. The warmth of dismissing the 'bad cop' to see his kid is a nice touch but doesn't quite land as a distinct joke moment. Functional background comedy.

jokes.text: Michael finds Buster at the police station just as he declared he was leaving to focus on his son. The awkward coincidence of Buster being right there as Michael exits.
jokes.setup: Michael has just declared 'It's all about my son' and started leaving
jokes.punchline: He immediately runs into Buster — the person he's been ostensibly not worrying about — making his 'I'm leaving' speech instantly ironic
jokes.explanation: Good structural irony — Michael's declaration of independence is immediately undercut by the universe. The timing of the coincidence is the joke. Functional AD plotting-as-comedy.

jokes.text: Briefly. You going to the family thing that we're... Giving to ourselves? Huh? Yeah. I, uh, can't make it. Yeah? No? Should I tell them? Well, you know, listen, I-I wasn't gonna go, so don't-don't not go, because I'm... you know. You're the reason I would go.
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael have just been reunited and immediately dance around an awkward conversation
jokes.punchline: The conversation is a cascade of non-committal almost-statements — both characters desperately avoiding expressing that they want to see each other — until 'You're the reason I would go' slips out as an inadvertent admission
jokes.explanation: The mutual evasion is classic Bluth family communication — both wanting to express affection and being constitutionally unable to. 'You're the reason I would go' landing as an accidental emotional admission is touching and funny. Strong character integration for both Michael and George Michael.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah? You got plans, huh? Well, we make plans, and God laughs. That's right. Right? No, I always want to make plans, but... You don't want to be laughed at.
jokes.setup: George Michael says he can't make the family event because he has plans
jokes.punchline: Michael deploys the aphorism 'We make plans and God laughs,' then George Michael twists it: 'No, I always want to make plans, but...' and Michael completes it with 'You don't want to be laughed at' — implying George Michael is afraid of divine mockery rather than uncertainty
jokes.explanation: The evolution of the 'God laughs' aphorism into 'you don't want to be laughed at' is clever — it reframes cosmic uncertainty as personal embarrassment anxiety, which perfectly characterizes both Bluths. Solid craft.

jokes.text: Only reason I ask, 'cause I'm going on a scuba trip. Um, yeah, came back in town to get my gear. Only reason I'm here, um, you know. It's a last-minute thing. Several weeks at sea. You'd hate it. So, yes.
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael are doing an awkward dance of not-inviting each other
jokes.punchline: 'You'd hate it. So, yes.' — Michael rapidly talks himself through a scuba trip invitation, decides his son would hate it, and closes the thought with 'So, yes' — confirming he's not inviting him while pretending the decision is on George Michael's behalf
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-interrupting non-invitation is a perfect piece of character comedy — he's simultaneously reaching out and withdrawing, and 'You'd hate it. So, yes.' closes the loop with devastating self-awareness. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I mean, so much of life is, uh, plans. It would just be the same joke over and over. So he's... It's not funny anymore. What's he laughing at?
jokes.setup: Michael has been riffing on 'God laughs at plans' and gets lost in his own logic
jokes.punchline: Michael concludes that God would be tired of laughing at plans by now, reducing the deity to a bored comedian whose material has gone stale — 'What's he laughing at?' as a genuine theological inquiry
jokes.explanation: Michael taking the God-laughs-at-plans metaphor seriously enough to wonder if God is bored with the joke is an amusing extension. The comedy is in his literal-minded theological deduction. Loses some economy points for being slightly drawn out.

jokes.text: The thing with, um... So, Rebel, you know, 'cause she... I wanted to say, between the two of you, you know, if you ever felt like... Because, like, she's indicated that things were... I don't know what she said, but maybe things were not quite wrapped up completely with... Between me and her?
jokes.setup: Michael tries to bring up Rebel Alley with George Michael
jokes.punchline: Michael's complete inability to complete a sentence about Rebel is the joke — a man who prides himself on directness reduced to pronoun soup by paternal guilt
jokes.explanation: Michael's verbal disintegration when trying to discuss Rebel is excellent cringe comedy — he's never this inarticulate, which signals how guilty he is. The economy suffers because the joke is its own length; the comedy is the extended fumbling rather than a punchline.

jokes.text: You know, I broke up with her. You know, I show up there, I got a cactus, for God's sakes, you know, which is... that's the universal sign for 'things are over.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Michael he broke up with Rebel
jokes.punchline: His evidence that he broke up with her: he brought a cactus, which he declares 'the universal sign for things are over' — there is absolutely no such convention; Michael invented this
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. 'A cactus is the universal sign for things are over' is a completely invented social convention delivered with utter sincerity by Michael. It's absurdist (no one thinks this), character-true (Michael's self-deluding confidence), and perfectly economical. The specificity of 'a cactus' is exactly right — not flowers, not a card, a cactus. Earns its near-9 impact because the room would erupt at the confident absurdity.

jokes.text: Well, if you ever change your mind, I ain't going nowhere. / Michael didn't know why he said the next thing out of his mouth. Maybe it was relief that he didn't have to break up with her, but for some reason, he came up with...
jokes.setup: Michael had been broken up with by Rebel; he then says 'Well, if you ever change your mind, I ain't going nowhere'
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes Michael had no idea why he said this — he was supposed to be breaking up with her, she broke up with him first, and his response was to make himself available — completely undermining his story to George Michael
jokes.explanation: The narrator calling out Michael's self-deception ('he didn't know why he said it') while Michael is confidently telling his son he broke up with her is a great structural joke. Michael's 'I ain't going nowhere' as a desperate retention play after being dumped is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Actually, the only contact George Michael had had with Rebel was watching one of her movies, with a Shane and three Noahs.
jokes.setup: Michael claims to have told Rebel their relationship is done
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals George Michael's 'contact' with Rebel was watching a movie with named characters — 'a Shane and three Noahs' implies a very specific screening party, possibly also a joke about trendy baby names
jokes.explanation: 'A Shane and three Noahs' is an amusingly specific description of a viewing party, possibly poking fun at millennial baby name trends. The joke works as ironic counterpoint to Michael's claim that George Michael is 'all in' on Rebel.

jokes.text: And you can say goodbye to these!
jokes.setup: George Michael watches one of Rebel Alley's movies with a group of people
jokes.punchline: A dramatic movie line ('And you can say goodbye to these!') causes George Michael to realize he misses her — the context implies she's saying goodbye to something meaningful in the film, and George Michael projects his own feelings onto it
jokes.explanation: The joke hinges on what 'these' refers to — in context it's likely a physical double entendre that lands with the viewing audience at George Michael's screening. The narrator's 'Maybe I do miss her' makes the joke explicit. Competent structure.

jokes.text: No, I told her. You know, I even said, 'Y'all are it for me.' You know. Go a step further and say, 'Y'all ain't going nowhere.' She's it. I don't know why she wouldn't think I was... wasn't going nowhere.
jokes.setup: Michael insists he made his commitment clear to Rebel
jokes.punchline: Michael's expression of commitment ('Y'all are it for me') uses 'Y'all' — addressing both Rebel and George Michael together as the objects of his affirmation, which is inadvertently including his son in a romantic declaration and revealing he's aware of the love triangle
jokes.explanation: Michael using 'Y'all' to Rebel while talking about her relationship with George Michael inadvertently includes his son in his romantic declaration — 'Y'all are it for me' directed at Rebel and George Michael simultaneously is a beautiful piece of subtext comedy. High character integration because Michael's blind spots are uniquely his.

jokes.text: 'Ciao.' / 'Bella.' — the elevator closes as Michael and George Michael part without resolving anything.
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael's awkward reunion ends with the elevator arriving
jokes.punchline: 'Ciao' / 'Bella' — the split Italian farewell is their version of 'goodbye,' pointlessly stylish and empty, perfectly capturing the unresolved nature of their relationship
jokes.explanation: The 'Ciao/Bella' callback is a perfect running gag payoff — two people completing a phrase that means nothing, as a substitute for anything real. The economy is nearly perfect (two words). Works especially well on rewatch.

jokes.text: That's it, I'm out of here! Michael returns for his bag. Okay, I get it. Is that supposed to be me? No, that's my impression of Tobias doing you.
jokes.setup: Michael dramatically declares he's leaving
jokes.punchline: Tobias is performing 'an impression of Tobias doing Michael' — a nested impression where Tobias is playing himself playing Michael, and Michael has to confirm whether he's the original or the impression
jokes.explanation: The recursion of 'an impression of Tobias doing you' — Tobias playing himself playing Michael — is a layered meta joke that the episode has been building toward. The confusion about which layer of performance we're watching mirrors the whole episode's identity confusion. Strong originality for how it resolves the episode's Tobias-as-Michael thread.

jokes.text: Tobias doing Tobias. Yep? / I don't care. I'm... All right.
jokes.setup: The nested impression confusion has resolved
jokes.punchline: Tobias confirms 'Tobias doing Tobias' as a performance category — acknowledging that being himself is also a performance — and Michael's resigned 'All right' accepts this insane framing
jokes.explanation: The confirmation that 'Tobias doing Tobias' is a distinct performance mode implies Tobias's authentic self is also a character he performs — a deeply strange and perfectly Arrested Development philosophical joke about identity and performance delivered in two words.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Tobias doing Tobias. Yep?' — delivered after the confusion about who is playing whom.
jokes.setup: The impression confusion has reached maximum density
jokes.punchline: 'Tobias doing Tobias' — the simplest possible resolution: in the end, he's always just playing himself. The 'Yep?' adds a beat of hopeful confirmation-seeking.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the recursive impression sequence. 'Tobias doing Tobias' is simultaneously the admission of failure and the deepest truth about the character — he can never truly be anyone else. The 'Yep?' is a masterful tiny addition: he needs validation even for this. Maximum economy, maximum character. Earns 8+ for being the ideal closing note on a complex joke sequence.

jokes.text: And Buster wonders why Michael isn't doing Michael. Why hasn't Michael come back? What happened to 'Michael always comes back'?
jokes.setup: The episode is ending with Buster alone, wondering where Michael is
jokes.punchline: 'What happened to Michael always comes back?' — both a character observation (Buster relying on Michael) and a meta-comment on the show's own premise (the narrator's opening 'the one son who had no choice but to keep them all together')
jokes.explanation: The closing joke works on two levels: character (Buster's abandonment anxiety) and meta (challenging the show's own premise of Michael-as-rescuer). 'Michael always comes back' echoes the opening narration and closes the episode with an ironic question mark over its own premise. Strong structural bookending.

tmdb_overview: The family unites behind Lindsay’s campaign, but Michael senses that something is amiss. Maeby has an idea that George-Michael likes a little too much.
insight: Gob's physical comedy carries a 75-point episode despite subversive humor faltering mid-season.
